name: Do I need a moisture barrier under my new floors in a coastal California home?
headline: Do I need a moisture barrier under my new floors in a coastal California home?

mainEntity.name: Do I need a moisture barrier under my new floors in a coastal California home?
mainEntity.text: Do I need a moisture barrier under my new floors in a coastal California home?

acceptedAnswer.text: In areas with moisture, like coastal towns or homes near the beach, a moisture barrier is often a very good idea under certain types of flooring. For concrete slabs, you usually need some type of moisture protection if you are installing hardwood, laminate, or some vinyl products. That might be a plastic sheet, a special underlayment with built-in moisture protection, or a liquid-applied moisture barrier. The exact product depends on the floor you are putting down and the manufacturer’s instructions. Over crawlspaces, building codes often require plastic over bare earth plus good ventilation. This helps keep moisture from moving up into the subfloor and into your flooring. Too much moisture can cause wood to swell, cup, or warp and can even affect glue-down products. You or your installer should test the subfloor for moisture before installation, especially on lower levels and in older homes. Many flooring warranties are only valid if you follow their moisture testing and barrier requirements. A common mistake is skipping moisture protection because the concrete “feels dry” or because the home is not technically in a flood zone. In coastal Bay Area climates, damp fog, driving rain, and older construction can still let moisture into slabs and crawlspaces. Following the flooring manufacturer’s moisture recommendations is the safest way to protect your investment.
